title: Why Your Mitochondria Are the Secret to Unstoppable Energy and Longevity
guestBio: Kristina Kristen is a lifestyle medicine educator, chemistry professor, and author of 10,000 Suns: The 28-Day Mitochondria Reset. She specializes in helping people optimize their cellular health through her NESTS Framework, combining her scientific background with practical wellness strategies.
excerpt: Discover how your mitochondria—your cells' powerhouses—control everything from energy to hormones to aging. Learn the NESTS Framework to reset your cellular engines and manage your health with the same strategic intention you bring to leadership.
metaDescription: Learn how to reset your mitochondria for more energy, better health, and longevity with Kristina Kristen's science-backed NESTS Framework.
keyTakeaways: Mitochondria function as your body's operating system, not just powerhouses, controlling energy, hormones, and aging processes.
The NESTS Framework—Nutrition, Exercise, Sleep, Toxin avoidance, and Stress control—provides a systematic approach to mitochondrial health.
Maternal mitochondrial DNA creates a unique health legacy that passes from mothers to children.
Women can apply the same strategic thinking they use in business to optimize their cellular health and energy levels.
Simple daily habits can dramatically improve mitochondrial function, leading to better fat burning, mental clarity, and longevity.
Toxin avoidance and stress management are equally important as nutrition and exercise for cellular health.
Resetting mitochondria requires a 28-day commitment but delivers lasting improvements in energy and vitality.

pullQuotes.text: Your mitochondria are your body's operating system—not just the powerhouses

timestamps.label: Understanding Mitochondria as Your Body's Operating System
Maternal Mitochondrial DNA and Health Legacy
Managing Health Like a Business Leader

content:
What if the secret to boundless energy, razor-sharp focus, and graceful aging isn't hiding in the latest supplement bottle or wellness trend? What if it's been inside you all along, waiting in the microscopic powerhouses of every single cell in your body?

In this transformative episode of The Manage Her, host Aimee Rickabus welcomes Kristina Kristen, lifestyle medicine educator, chemistry professor, and author of 10,000 Suns: The 28-Day Mitochondria Reset, for a deep dive into the fascinating world of cellular health. This isn't just another wellness conversation—it's a masterclass in understanding how your mitochondria shape every aspect of your existence, from your morning energy levels to your long-term health legacy.

Your Mitochondria: The Ultimate Operating System

You've probably heard mitochondria called the powerhouses of the cell, but Kristina reveals a more profound truth: they're actually your body's operating system. Just as your smartphone needs a robust operating system to run all its applications smoothly, your body relies on healthy mitochondria to manage everything from hormone production to mood regulation to mental clarity.

Think about it this way—when your phone's operating system is outdated or corrupted, every app runs slowly, crashes frequently, or simply doesn't work. The same principle applies to your mitochondria. When these cellular engines aren't functioning optimally, you experience fatigue, brain fog, weight gain, and accelerated aging. But when they're running efficiently? You feel unstoppable.

This perspective shift changes everything about how you approach your health. Instead of chasing symptoms with quick fixes, you're addressing the root cause at the cellular level. You're not just managing your energy—you're optimizing your body's fundamental operating system.

The NESTS Framework: Your Blueprint for Cellular Excellence

Kristina introduces her groundbreaking NESTS Framework—a systematic approach that stands for Nutrition, Exercise, Sleep, Toxin avoidance, and Stress control. This isn't another overwhelming wellness protocol; it's a strategic framework you can implement with the same methodical approach you bring to your business leadership.

The beauty of the NESTS Framework lies in its comprehensive yet manageable approach. Nutrition focuses on feeding your mitochondria the specific nutrients they need to thrive, not just following generic diet advice. Exercise becomes about stimulating mitochondrial biogenesis—literally creating new, healthy mitochondria. Sleep transforms from a luxury into a non-negotiable investment in cellular repair and regeneration.

Perhaps most importantly, the framework addresses toxin avoidance and stress control—two factors that many wellness approaches overlook but that can sabotage even the best nutrition and exercise efforts. Kristina emphasizes that mitochondria are particularly vulnerable to environmental toxins and chronic stress, making these elements crucial to your cellular health strategy.

The Maternal Mitochondrial Legacy

One of the most compelling aspects of mitochondrial health is its unique inheritance pattern. Unlike most of your DNA, which comes from both parents, mitochondrial DNA passes exclusively from mother to child. This creates a powerful health legacy that every woman carries—and passes on.

Understanding this maternal connection adds profound meaning to your wellness journey. When you invest in your mitochondrial health, you're not just improving your own energy and longevity—you're potentially impacting the health foundation you pass to your children. This knowledge transforms self-care from selfish indulgence to generational responsibility.

For the high-achieving women listening to The Manage Her, this concept resonates deeply. You understand legacy building in business and leadership; now you can apply that same intentional legacy thinking to your cellular health. The energy and vitality you cultivate today becomes part of the inheritance you leave tomorrow.

Strategic Health Management for Leaders

What sets this episode apart is how Kristina and Aimee connect mitochondrial health to leadership effectiveness. As women who manage teams, businesses, and families, you already understand systems thinking, strategic planning, and consistent execution. These same skills apply perfectly to managing your cellular health.

Kristina encourages you to approach your mitochondrial reset with the same intentionality you bring to your professional goals. Set clear objectives, track your progress, and adjust your strategy based on results. This isn't about perfection—it's about consistent, strategic action that compounds over time.

The 28-day mitochondrial reset that Kristina outlines in her book provides a structured timeline, similar to a business project with clear milestones and deliverables. You can measure improvements in energy levels, sleep quality, mental clarity, and physical performance, giving you concrete data about your cellular health investment.

Daily Habits That Transform Cellular Function

The most encouraging aspect of mitochondrial health is how responsive these cellular engines are to positive changes. Unlike some health improvements that take months or years to manifest, mitochondrial optimization can begin showing results within days or weeks of implementing the right strategies.

Kristina emphasizes that small, consistent actions create dramatic improvements in mitochondrial function. This might include specific timing of meals to optimize cellular energy production, incorporating movement patterns that stimulate new mitochondria creation, or implementing stress management techniques that protect existing mitochondria from damage.

The key is understanding that your mitochondria are constantly adapting to your lifestyle choices. Every meal, every workout, every hour of sleep, and every stress management technique sends signals to your cells about what kind of energy system they need to build and maintain.

Your Permission Slip to Prioritize Cellular Health

As women leaders, you excel at taking care of others—your teams, your families, your communities. But often, you put your own health last, treating self-care as something you'll get to when you have time. This episode gives you permission to reframe that thinking entirely.

Optimizing your mitochondrial health isn't selfish—it's strategic leadership. When your cellular engines are running efficiently, you show up with more energy, clearer thinking, better mood regulation, and increased resilience. You become a more effective leader, a more present mother, and a more powerful force for positive change in your sphere of influence.

Kristina's approach removes the guilt from health optimization by positioning it as essential infrastructure for everything else you want to accomplish. Just as you wouldn't try to run a business on unreliable technology, you can't sustain high-level leadership on compromised cellular function.

This conversation between Aimee and Kristina provides both the scientific foundation and practical tools you need to transform your energy, health, and longevity from the cellular level up. It's time to stop managing your symptoms and start optimizing your operating system. Your mitochondria—and your legacy—are waiting.

quiz.title: What's Your Energy Operating System?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach your health and energy—and unlock the cellular secrets to feeling unstoppable.

questions.question: When you're feeling consistently tired, what's your typical first response?

options.text: Research the root causes and create a systematic plan to address them
Try a few different approaches and see what feels right for your body
Make small, gradual changes to your daily routine and stick with them
Look for quick wins that fit easily into your current schedule

questions.question: How do you prefer to learn about new wellness approaches?

options.text: Deep-dive into scientific studies and evidence-based frameworks
Listen to personal stories and try things that resonate intuitively
Focus on proven methods with clear, step-by-step implementation
Seek practical tips that I can easily integrate without major disruption

questions.question: What motivates you most when it comes to taking care of your health?

options.text: Understanding exactly how and why something works at a cellular level
Feeling energized and aligned with what my body is telling me
Building sustainable habits that will benefit me for years to come
Having more energy to show up fully for the people and projects I love

questions.question: When starting a new health routine, you typically:

options.text: Create a detailed plan with measurable goals and tracking systems
Start with what feels most appealing and adjust based on how you feel
Begin with one small change and build momentum slowly over time
Focus on the changes that require the least disruption to your current life

questions.question: Your biggest challenge with maintaining healthy habits is:

options.text: Getting overwhelmed by all the conflicting information out there
Sticking with something long enough to see if it really works
Staying motivated when results don't come as quickly as you'd like
Finding time and energy when you're already stretched thin

questions.question: When you think about your long-term health and aging, you:

options.text: Want to understand the science behind what influences longevity and vitality
Trust that taking care of yourself now will naturally lead to better outcomes
Focus on building a foundation of habits that will serve you for decades
Hope to find sustainable strategies that don't require constant attention

results.title: The Strategic Optimizer
results.description: You approach your health like a well-planned business strategy—methodical, research-driven, and always looking for systems that deliver results. You appreciate frameworks and love understanding the 'why' behind wellness recommendations. Your analytical mind is your superpower, but you might sometimes get stuck in the planning phase. This episode will give you the scientific blueprint you crave with Kristina's NESTS Framework, helping you channel your strategic thinking into a systematic approach to cellular health.

results.title: The Intuitive Experimenter
results.description: You're naturally curious about wellness trends and love trying new approaches to feel your best. You trust your body's signals and aren't afraid to experiment with different strategies. Your openness to new ideas is a gift, but you might benefit from a more structured approach to lasting change. This episode will help you understand the foundational science behind why certain wellness practices work, giving you the framework to make your intuitive choices even more powerful.

results.title: The Steady Builder
results.description: You believe in consistency over perfection and prefer making gradual, sustainable changes rather than dramatic overhauls. You understand that real transformation takes time, and you're willing to put in the work for long-term results. Your patience and commitment are incredible strengths. This episode will resonate deeply with you, especially Kristina's 28-day mitochondria reset approach, which aligns perfectly with your belief in steady, meaningful progress.

results.title: The Busy Balancer
results.description: You're juggling multiple priorities and need health solutions that fit into your packed schedule without adding complexity. You're incredibly efficient and resourceful, but you might sometimes put your own energy needs on the back burner. Your ability to multitask is impressive, but your body deserves the same attention you give everything else. This episode will show you how simple daily habits can dramatically impact your cellular health without overwhelming your already full plate.

transcript: Let's [music] >> Hi everyone and welcome back to the manage her the podcast where we turn the invisible work of women into powerful intentional leadership. I'm your host Amy Rickabus, author of The Manager, entrepreneur and mama of six. Today's guest is someone I've admired for years, not just for her mind, but for the way she lives her message. Christina Christristen is a lifestyle medicine educator, chemistry professor, writer, and health mentor devoted to helping people reclaim their energy and vitality through conscious daily choices. Her groundbreaking work in mitochondrial science bridges decades of chemistry and biology with practical realorld habits that anyone can apply. Christina's first book, Beyond Just Genes, explored how our mitochondria shape every aspect of health. And in her newest book, 10,000 Sons: The 28day Mitochondria Reset, she gives us the road map to restore our body's cellular engines, burn fat, sharpen focus, and age with strength and vitality. She says mitochondria aren't just the powerhouses of your cells. They're the operating system of your body. And when they're depleted, everything from your hormones to your mood to your mental clarity starts to suffer. Today we're talking about how to manage your health the way you manage your business with intention, intelligence, and the right tools. Hi Christina, thanks for coming today. >> Thank you so much for having me, Amy. And right back at you when I could triple what you just said about my ad admiration for you throughout the years. You're just unbelievably prolific and wise and just an amazing person. So, thank you for for that intro and thank you for having me. >> Absolutely. So glad you're here. Let's start with the science of energy. I love how you say mitochondria are the operating system of the body, not just the powerhouses. Can you explain that concept for our listeners who might be hearing this for the first time? >> Yes. You know, so for the longest time, the [clears throat] mitochondria have had the title of the powerhouses of the cell, sometimes the engines of the cell, but basically this has been their title for decades now. And specifically in the last two decades, what has transpired is this much deeper understanding of their role within the cell. And they are incredibly influential and important in every aspect of the cell. I could roll off like 20 different you know very specific roles but I would say the most important ones are obviously the energy generation which is foundational. We you know life is energy the flow of energy. So that is foundational but beyond that they control metabolism. So whether you're burning fat or sugar, so extremely important in weight and metabolism, they determine whether the cell lives or uh commits basically suicide, a term called apoptosis. And this is so important not only because as we know cancer is uncontrolled proliferation but in every way you need to basically get rid of damaged cells in order for the health and well-being of the good cells to continue so that the resources go towards the the healthy cells. So this vital role of determining whether the cell lives or dies is within the mitochondria. So I think that's next to energy transformation. That's their next most important role. They also produce your sex hormones. So estrogen, testosterone, cortisol. They also they give you your body heat. They're thermmore regulators. You know, they turn they're very very foundational in epigenetics. So they turn your genes. It's not that you're just born with a set of genes that are just, you know, basically static. They determine through your lifestyle which genes get turned on and off. So, I can just go on and on, but I think it's clear that they're not just the energy producing elements within your cells. They're literally the operating system. That's amazing. It's such a different take on mitochondria. I always knew they were important, but not this important. You know, we'll never look at them the same way again. Absolutely. knowing that that they're really regulating so many of our aspects of our bodies. >> Yeah, very much. >> That's amazing. >> Quite frankly, I think for me, the more deeply you look into it, the more you really sort of pull back the layers. For me, what really inspired me to write these books is most people not only don't know the importance of their mitochondria, they've never even heard of their mitochondria. And I find this to be such a disconnect in terms of how do you decipher what is elemental to your health if you haven't even heard of the most foundational entities which are those that are producing your energy and basically operating the entire they're like the telecommunication network you know that's basically running the show. How can we not know this? >> Yes. Yes. Totally. I mean, I was a bio nerd, so I definitely always paid attention to mitochondria, but most people, you know, I don't know. They mitochondria are the unsung heroes of the body. We know about all of the larger organ systems of the body, but I don't think people are really looking at intracellular function as a major cause of disease in the body. And now we're looking at mitochondria as that, you know, very small thing that's actually so foundationally important to that human health. So, you've taught chemistry for years. How do you translate complex science into something people can actually live daytoday? >> It's a muscle. It's truly a muscle that you want to meet people where they're at. You don't want to overwhelm them and you want to be able to relay the information in a way that makes sense. Also doesn't dumb it down. I think that just from, you know, my love is chemistry. Actually, I find it to be incredibly profound. I've actually been brought to tears by some of the realizations throughout the years of just how important some of the most foundational, you know, the biological chemical formulas are. So, for me, it's really this passion and love to be able to make a bridge between something that may be a little bit too nerdy or complex and bringing that to life. Actually, in my classes, what I really love to do is connect what we're learning to the real world around you. like why are we learning this? How is this important? And so I I think that that's that's something that I try my best to do in the best way possible so that it doesn't lose its meaning and is it hits people in the right spot. >> Yeah, I love that. I love that. So you often say that when we don't have health, we can't serve. Why is that connection between vitality and purpose so important to you? You know, so when you're in an airplane and heaven forbid you're told, you know, this is something that we've all grown up with, that if heaven forbid something happens, you need to put the oxygen mask on first. You don't put that oxygen mask on anyone other than yourself first. Why? Because >> you're pretty much not going to be useful if you're not there, right? If you pass out. So essentially I think aside from time our health is one of the most if not the most important asset that we have. >> So to take care of our own health is foundational so that we can also contribute to the society around us and to to basically help others in my case also be healthy. But whatever your passion or your purpose is you can't really do it when you're sick as well. >> It's so true. So true. Yeah. You know, as I've been writing my next book, I'm talking about success and happiness and all the aspects of happiness in our life. And it's the one that always tends to come back up is health. How health pertains to happiness in human happiness, it impacts it in such a major way. So, I feel like, you know, as we're having this discussion about managing your health, you know, when you are well, it's much easier to do the things you want to do. It's much easier to live the life you want to be, be the mom you want to be, be the business woman you want to be. Taking care of ourselves, man, man managing our health is one of the essential things we have to do as the managers of ourselves, as our managers, you know. So, I love that your new book, 10,000 Sons, introduces a 28-day mitochondria reset. What inspired that title and what does it mean to have 10,000 sons inside of us? >> Oh my gosh, thank you for that question. I love that question. I loved this notion of let me just explain the title 10,000 Sons. So I I read this incredibly interesting, fascinating book by uh Nick Lane. It's called uh Power, Sex, and Suicide. And um basically he's a famous uh prolific author, an evolutionary biologist, and his expertise is in mitochondria. So his name is Nick Lane. Anyway, so within this book, he did a calculation which struck me as so profound and he said that gram per gram that we uh within us we outperform the sun by 10,000 times in energy transformation and the efficiency with which we transform energy. Can you imagine that? I mean the sun is it's the life force, right? The external life force. Well, the internal life force that we have is even more efficient than the iconic, right, the energy um source that we have on this planet. So, I thought it grabbed me so much this 10,000 suns idea that I thought, "Oh my gosh, I want to have that be my title." >> I love that. You really don't think about that. You really don't think of the conversion of energy that happens within the human body. >> It's incredible, you know, how truly it's like a miracle. Right? It's it's trillions of miracle that happen every second within us, right? And what our mitochondria contribute to that in essence. And the second part to your question was when I wrote beyond just genes, I had so many people reach me uh try to reach me to say like what do I do specifically like you know do I do this? What do I do with my diet? What do I do with my exercise? What do I, you know, what are the ways that I can really have an impact on my health? And it just was such a natural no-brainer next step for me to create the protocol that is based on actual sciencebacked peer-reviewed published studies of the most important things that you can do within your day that really impact the health of your mitochondria. The one thing I want to say is, you know, why 28 days? I could have said 21 days, but I just thought 28 days somehow sounded I I wanted to use 28, but I could have said 21. And the reason for that is, tell me if you've heard this, but we've always been told that it takes three weeks to get into shape, right? And one week to get out of shape. So that's what I grew up with. It was always like, oh, it takes three weeks to get into shape. My understanding now is that >> yes, it's about your muscles. It's about training and all that, but really it's about your mitochondria because within that 3 weeks of doing the let's just say the hormesis exercises, things that for an acute amount of time they stress your mitochondria and they demand more energy and your mitochondria have to produce more of themselves and they have to become more efficient. So that three weeks that it takes for you to get into shape, it's really foundationally about your mitochondria getting into shape. >> Wow. >> So that's why what you do in 3 weeks, four weeks, 28 days, if you do it consistently, is transformative. >> Wow. >> Wow. That's amazing, Christina. That is so amazing. And then use an acronym nests. Could you walk me through that? How each one of those Yeah. what it is and talk us through your pillars for to support your mitochondrial health. >> So I wanted to make it easy, memorable and something that people can kind of hold on to. And so there are pillars of health that you know when you take you know any sort of training in health people break it down into the pillars. So one pillar is nutrition, exercise, sleep, toxin avoidance and stress management. Another one is community building. Okay. So for me, I really wanted to focus on the five. Nutrition, exercise, sleep, toxin avoidance, and stress control. So I made this pneumonic nests. And within these pillars, these five pillars, the nests program, every single day in the 10,000 sons, my 28-day mitochondria reset, what you're doing is you're sending all the beneficial signals through your nutrition, exercise, sleep, toxin avoidance, and stress control to your mitochondria, which amplifies the signals in a way that if you just were to do one and not the other four, it's still benefits you, but it benefits you exponentially more if you're doing all five and then doing them consistently and then doing them with a midfocused mindsets. So, you know, very specifically saying, does this serve my mitochondria? What do I need to eliminate? What do I need to add? What can I do better? And I tell you I know what my for for example within those five pillars for me I need to do better you know so we all wherever you are wherever you are you're going to some people have a peak in one pillar but not the other >> or they have you know there we do our best but the point is you can basically enter this sort of how to optimize your health wherever you are. >> Amazing amazing I really love it. I I find myself going back to it going, "Oh, wait. Nest. Okay, am I doing it? Okay, I know I need to work on more exercise." It's one of the things as I've gotten busier with everything. It's the one thing that's been highly compromised [laughter] in my situation. Like, oh my gosh, that's the one. I love sleep. I can never give up. My sleep is a it's like a non-negotiable and nutrition is a non-negotiable. Um, but man, exercise has been a little negotiable for me lately. Um, >> well, [laughter] you know what's also interesting, Amy, is that what you discover because if you're really fascinated with longevity and you look into the real world data, the exercise can be as simple as walking, you know, just getting your steps in. I know that sounds like ridiculously simple, but you look at the blue zones, they're not going to the gym. They're not going and doing a million different like Pilates and you know like uh HIT and all the you know I mean I'm not saying that you shouldn't be doing that. I think it's a phenomenal thing to do but in the proof is in the pudding sort of the best scientific data that we have is the real world data of the blue zones and really what they do every day every single day it's baked into their routines is they walk. That's it. So I hope that like you know for me I'm just like all right okay I walked today then [laughter] >> I walked my 500 miles >> I'm like yeah how yeah so 5,000 steps for you then is what you're like aiming for >> I mean I'm aiming for the you know hopefully 7,000 to 10,000 but it also doesn't have to be all at once that's the other thing in fact the data is coming out that if you can do it even a little bit at a time is usually better let's Just say you only have x amount of time and that's the only time you can do the walking, that's fine. But if you can take breaks throughout your day to do five minutes, 10 minutes, that has even more impact than doing it all at once. They're both good. But just so for for those who say, I don't have more time, everybody has five minutes. Everybody has 10 minutes, right? >> You eat, you can go for a walk for five minutes hopefully. Anyway, so it's just way to like >> make not make it so daunting. It's not so daunting. >> I love that. I love that. Yeah, because if it Yeah, because it is something I can do, you know, go eat dinner and then go for a quick walk around the neighborhood, right? >> That's the Mediterranean. Get your steps in. >> The Mediterranean lifestyle. A lot of the blue zones, Sardinia, Greece, you know, they have it. Like I said, the walking is baked in. They eat and then they walk. You walk everywhere. >> Yes. Those are the places in the world where people live the longest. >> Yes. >> That's amazing. So for the women balancing work and motherhood and leadership, where do you recommend they start and what's one small shift that can create a big impact? >> Other than just take going for a walk. I love that one. >> I love that. Um I I think that the awareness is number one. I kind of make an analogy. Let's just say you have a desire. You're in point A. You're in point A and you want to get to point B. Let's just say that's the case. and you know, you're given a map, but on that map, there's no point B. There are all these other points, but there's no point B. You're not going to get to point B unless it's by accident. The idea that I'm suggesting is that once you recognize that energy production that meets the demands of your cells is the most important thing to pay attention to for anything if you want to avoid getting chronic illnesses or you know boost your immune system etc. So the awareness I would say that you look into what supports your mitochondria, you look into what doesn't support your mitochondria and then on a daily basis it's a compounded nothing in one day is going to do anything right the effort of doing the right things over years that add health span and lifespan to your to your existence. So I would say for women who want to start, if you think about it as in the most foundational, let's just say simplified version, two buckets. You have the good bucket and the bad bucket. Start to remove the things that are not beneficial for your mitochondria, the processed foods, excess sugar, the seed oils. Try to minimize your stress level. You know, the chronic stress is incredibly detrimental to your health. Each of those pillars that I mentioned, different women, it's going to mean different things. Maybe they're doing really well with the nutritional part, but they're not doing so well with the exercise part. Or maybe they're doing those two well, but they're sleeping not very well. So, you need to kind of take inventory of your five pillars and say, "Hey, if I want to live to be healthy like a Blue Zone member, what can I do now on a daily basis that's sustainable?" That's the most important thing. like any one of us can do something for a short amount of time. I think each one of us knows what we can sustain. So try to choose those you know daily interventions that you think fit within your sensibilities, your sense of comfort, what you can sustain and really do that over time and it's just inevitable that you will see a transformation. So, I'm sorry I'm not being very specific, but I would say, you know, adopt a Mediterranean diet. Get rid of all the processed foods. Get rid of any sort of toxins that you're being exposed to, whether it's your personal products. We know where the toxic loads. Sometimes we know where they are and sometimes we can't control them. To the extent that we can control that, get rid of the toxic load or diminish it, lessen it because you don't want to handcuff your mitochondria and they are 10 times more susceptible to toxins than our own cells. So our mitochondria really suffer from a toxic load. So you really want to minimize that so you're not handcuffing them and then add to the good things the you know the exercise the diet the stress control cortisol is incredibly as I said cortisol which is a stress hormone that revs up your mitochondria and for a short amount of time revving up your mitochondria that's very very important to you know in the past when we were running away from tigers or some very acute threat some something that we need to run away from the defense mechanism was for your mitochondria to create cortisol. And the cortisol basically it's like putting the throttle, you know, if you're going 10 miles an hour, it's like all of a sudden going 90 miles hour. That is important when you're trying to run away from something. But in today's life to be going at 90 miles hour cortisol all day long, that ages us and that's incredibly it's not a healthy way to live. So you know anything that gives brings you peace like meditation, yoga, walking, walking, reading, whatever brings you a state of clarity and takes the foot off the gas for your mitochondria. >> Yeah, totally. Yeah, I love getting in the garden. It's like, you know, sometimes it's get let me play in the dirt for a few minutes. I'll feel fine. Or just walk in walk in the sand of the beach. You know, those are things that help me shift. Yes. >> Yeah. shift out of that stress hormone and into, you know, let's calm it down a little bit. [laughter] [gasps] >> Yes. Totally. So, you've said mitochondria don't just make energy, they make decisions. What are some signs that our cells are struggling or that we're running on a low voltage? Yes, I think that's a very very key question because like everything else, the earlier you catch something that's going wrong, the more you have the ability to stop or reverse whatever it is that's happening. So, brain fog, constant fatigue, a feeling that you're just not well all the time. Now, we all have these, you know, we're not machines, so we have these sort of interspersed throughout weeks that we may not feel great one day or another day, but if it's really a chronic situation where you're feeling like you don't have energy, you have brain fog, you can't really think clearly, you get every cold or flu, your immune system is is not really optimal, you're all of a sudden gaining weight, and you're you don't understand, you're not really doing anything differently, but somehow you're just gaining weight. your metabolic numbers like your blood sugar when if you do some blood work your blood sugar numbers are you know becoming elevated so you're pre-diabetic or diabetic. So these are all the telltale signs that your mitochondria are not really being served optimally. They're not functioning at their best. In fact, like a lot of people these days, you know, you hear metabolic, metabolic, metabolic, metabolic illnesses, but metabolic is mitochondria. Metabolism is mitochondria. So, metabolic illnesses like heart disease, even cancer, diabetes, as I said, even dementia, these are all metabolic illnesses. Metabolism is in mitochondria disconnect that people don't fully connect those two. So essentially the tellt tell signs are that you're not feeling well in one capacity or another and it's chronic. It's really great to pay attention to that and not try to just crush the symptoms and not just to turn off the symptoms because for a short amount of time that's okay. But if your goal is to not have that spiral into further symptoms, it's better to get at the root causes and nurture your mitochondria so that you reestablish your optimal health. >> I love that. Yeah, I've really struggled especially in my in my mid20s. I was diagnosed with metabolic syndrome, polycystic ovarian syndrome, Hashimoto's, a heart murmur, all of the above. Like talk about fullon mitochondria crashing. What really helped me recover? I really had to have a holistic approach to what I called being a human diet. So I really had to change my diet back to eating really just whole foods, lots of green smoothies and um adopting actually really started walking a lot.
